name: flask-monitoringdashboard

metadata.name: Flask-MonitoringDashboard
classifiers: Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules
metadata.summary: Automatically monitor the evolving performance of Flask/Python web services.

documentation:
# Flask Monitoring Dashboard

A dashboard for automatic monitoring of Flask (<https://flask.palletsprojects.com>) web-services.

## Key Features

The Flask Monitoring Dashboard is an extension for Flask applications that offers four main functionalities with little effort from the Flask developer:

- **Monitor the performance and utilization:**
  The Dashboard allows you to see which endpoints process a lot of requests and how fast.
  Additionally, it provides information about the evolving performance of an endpoint throughout different versions if you're using git.
- **Profile requests and endpoints:**
  The execution path of every request is tracked and stored into the database. This allows you to gain
  insight over which functions in your code take the most time to execute. Since all requests for an
  endpoint are also merged together, the Dashboard provides an overview of which functions are used in
  which endpoint.
- **Collect extra information about outliers:**
  Outliers are requests that take much longer to process than regular requests.
  The Dashboard automatically detects that a request is an outlier and stores extra information about it (stack trace, request values, Request headers, Request environment).
- **Collect additional information about your Flask-application:**
  Suppose you have an User-table and you want to know how many users are registered on your Flask-application.
  Then, you can run the following query: 'SELECT Count(\*) FROM USERS;'. But this is just annoying to do regularly.
  Therefore, you can configure this in the Flask-MonitoringDashboard, which will provide you this information per day (or other time interval).

The dashboard is automatically added to your existing Flask application.
You can view the results by default using the default endpoint (this can be configured to another route):

> <http://localhost:5000/dashboard>

For more advanced documentation, take a look at the information
on [this site]

# How to use

## Installation

To install from source, download the source code, then run this:

> python setup.py install

Or install with pip:

> pip install flask_monitoringdashboard

## Setup

Adding the extension to your Flask app is simple:

> from flask import Flask
> import flask_monitoringdashboard as dashboard
>
> app = Flask(\_\_name\_\_)
> dashboard.bind(app)

## Live Demo

To view a live deployment of the Flask-MonitoringDashboard, [check this site]

Use the credentials u:`admin`, p:`admin` to log in.

## Feedback

In order to improve our Flask-MonitoringDashboard, we would like to hear from you! Therefore, we made a questionnaire
with a few questions. Filling in this form takes less than 3 minutes. You can find the form [here](https://goo.gl/forms/IqRrjGDDXe44q5ZV2).

Alternatively, feel free to write to [our email-address](mailto:flask.monitoringdashboard@gmail.com).

## Documentation

For more advanced documentation, [see this site](http://flask-monitoringdashboard.readthedocs.io)
If you run into trouble migrating from version 1.X.X to version 2.0.0, this site will help you solve this too.
The migration from 2.X.X to 3.0.0 should be easier.

## License

This project is licensed under the MIT License - see the LICENSE file for details.

# Change Log

All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.
This project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](http://semver.org/).
Please note that the changes before version 1.10.0 have not been documented.

## v3.3.2

Changed

- Changed functools.cache to lru_cache to support python 3.8
- Added python version to telemetry

## v3.3.1

Changed

- Telemetry now uses dynamic server-ip
- Removed survey
- FollowUp questions refactored

## v3.3.0

Changed

- Added database pruning feature

## v3.2.2

Changed

- Fixed Sphinx documentation

## v3.2.1

Changed

- Removed sentry artifacts in code

## v3.2.0

Changed

- Upgraded multiple frontend packages for enhanced security and performance.
- Added survey alert
- Added telemetry alert and functionality

## v3.1.2

Changed

- Compatibility with Flask>=2.x Removed the call to `before_app_first_request` and replaced it with `record_once` as per the PR of \[@FlorianRhiem\](<https://github.com/FlorianRhiem>).

## v3.1.0

Changed

- Added support for Python 3.8
- Started using pytest instead of python's unittest
- Started using Webpack for frontend packaging
- Improved reports
- Moved to Github Actions from Travis for CI/CD
- Improved docstrings
- Various bug fixes

## v3.0.9

Changed

- Fixed upgrade message bug
- Fixed Heroku deployment

## v3.0.8

Changed

- Fixed the changelog; functionality is the same as 3.0.7 :)

## v3.0.7

Changed

- Added a first version of the Reporting functionality
- Improved usability of the overview table
- Fixed the issue with some table columns being sorted as text as opposed to numbers
- A few other bug fixes

## v3.0.6

Changed

- Removed profiler feature from monitoring level 2
- Added outlier detection feature to monitoring level 3
- Configurable profiler sampling period, with 5 ms default
- Implemented an in-memory cache for performance improvements

## v3.0.0

Changed

- Tracking also status codes
- Display times as numbers to make them sortable
- Add leading slash to blueprint paths
- Added status codes with corresponding views

## v2.1.1

Changed

- Default monitoring level is now 1
- Fixed bug causing config file not being parsed
- Monitoring level can be set from the 'detail' section
- Improved README

## v2.1.0

Changed

- Frontend is now using AngularJS
- Removed TestMonitor
- Added Custom graphs
- Fixed Issue #206
- Added support for Python 3.7
- Updated documentation
- Updated unit tests

## v2.0.7

Changed

- Fixed Issue #174
- Fixed issue with profiler not going into code
- Implemented a Sunburst visualization of the Grouped Profiler
- Improved test coverage
- Improved python-doc
- Added functionality to download the outlier data
- Dropped support for Python 3.3 and 3.4

## v2.0.0

Changed

- Added a configuration option to prefix a table in the database
- Optimize queries, such that viewing data is faster
- Updated database scheme
- Implemented functionality to customize time window of graphs
- Implemented a profiler for Request profiling
- Implemented a profiler for Endpoint profiling
- Refactored current code, which improves readability
- Refactoring of Test-Monitoring page
- Identify testRun by Travis build number

## v1.13.0

Changed

- Added boxplot of CPU loads
- Updated naming scheme of all graphs
- Implemented two configuration options: the local timezone and the option to automatically monitor new endpoints
- Updated the Test-Monitoring initialization
- Updated Database support for MySQL

## v1.12.0

Changed

- Removed two graphs: hits per hour and execution time per hour
- New template design
- Refactored backhand of the code
- Updated Bootstrap 3.0 to 4.0
- Setup of Code coverage

## v1.11.0

Changed

- Added new graph: Version usage
- Added column (Hits in past 7 days) in Measurements Overview
- Fixed bug with configuration
- Changed rows and column in outlier-table
- Added TODO List
- Updated functionality to retrieve the stacktrace of an Outlier
- Fixed bug with white colors from the config option

## v1.10.0

Changed

- Added security for automatic endpoint-data retrieval.
- Added test for export_data-endpoints
- Added MIT License.
- Added documentation

[check this site]: https://flask-monitoringdashboard.herokuapp.com/
[this site]: _http://flask-monitoringdashboard.readthedocs.io/en/latest/functionality.html

modules.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.stack_line
documentation: Contains all functions that access an StackLine object.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.stack_line.add_stack_line
documentation:
Adds a StackLine to the database (and possibly a CodeLine)
\:param session: Session for the database
\:param request_id: id of the request
\:param position: position of the StackLine
\:param indent: indent-value
\:param duration: duration of this line (in ms)
\:param code_line: quadruple that consists of: (filename, line_number, function_name, code)

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.stack_line.get_profiled_requests
documentation:
Gets the requests of an endpoint sorted by request time, together with the stack lines.
\:param session: session for the database
\:param endpoint_id: filter profiled requests on this endpoint
\:param offset: number of items to skip
\:param per_page: number of items to return
\:return: A list with tuples. Each tuple consists first of a Request-object, and the second part
of the tuple is a list of StackLine-objects.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.stack_line.get_grouped_profiled_requests
documentation:
Gets the grouped stack lines of all requests of an endpoint.
\:param session: session for the database
\:param endpoint_id: filter profiled requests on this endpoint
\:return: A list with tuples. Each tuple consists first of a Request-object, and the second part
of the tuple is a list of StackLine-objects.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.code_line.get_code_line
documentation:
Get a CodeLine object from a given quadruple of fn, ln, name, code. If the CodeLine object
doesn't already exist, a new one is created in the database.
\:param session: session for the database
\:param fn: filename (string)
\:param ln: line_number of the code (int)
\:param name: function name (string)
\:param code: line of code (string)
\:return: a CodeLine object

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.custom_graph.get_graph_id_from_name
documentation:
---
param name: name of the graph (must be unique)
param session: session for the database
return: |-
  the graph_id corresponding to the name. If the name does not exists in the db,
  a new graph is added to the database.
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.custom_graph.get_graph_data
documentation:
---
param end_date: Datetime object that denotes the end of the interval
param graph_id: id to filter on
param session: session for the database
param start_date: Datetime object that denotes the beginning of the interval
return: A list with values retrieved from the database
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.count.count_rows
documentation:
Count the number of rows of a specified column.
\:param session: session for the database
\:param column: column to count
\:param criterion: where-clause of the query
\:return: number of rows

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.count.count_requests
documentation:
Return the number of hits for a specific endpoint (possible with more filter arguments).
\:param session: session for the database
\:param endpoint_id: id of the endpoint
\:param where: additional arguments

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.count.count_total_requests
documentation:
Return the number of total hits
\:param session: session for the database
\:param where: additional arguments

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.count.count_outliers
documentation:
---
param endpoint_id: id of the endpoint
param session: session for the database
return: An integer with the number of rows in the Outlier-table.
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.count.count_profiled_requests
documentation:
Count the number of profiled requests for a certain endpoint
\:param session: session for the database
\:param endpoint_id: id of the endpoint
\:return: An integer

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.data_grouped.get_data_grouped
documentation:
Return the data for a specific endpoint. The result is grouped on column
\:param session: session for the database
\:param column: the column that is used for grouping
\:param func: the function to reduce the data
\:param where: additional where clause

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.data_grouped.group_result
documentation:
---
param func: the function to reduce the data e.g. func=median
param result: 'A list of rows from the database: e.g. [(key, data1), (key, data2)]'
return: the data that is reduced. e.g. [(key, (data1+data2)/2)]
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.data_grouped.group_result_endpoint
documentation:
---
param func: the function to reduce the data e.g. func=median
param result: 'A list of rows from the database: e.g. [(key, data1), (key, data2)]'
return: the data that is reduced. e.g. [(key, (data1+data2)/2)]
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.data_grouped.get_endpoint_data_grouped
documentation:
---
param func: the function to reduce the data
param session: session for the database
param where: additional where clause
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.data_grouped.get_version_data_grouped
documentation:
---
param func: the function to reduce the data
param session: session for the database
param where: additional where clause
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.data_grouped.get_user_data_grouped
documentation:
---
param func: the function to reduce the data
param session: session for the database
param where: additional where clause
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.data_grouped.get_two_columns_grouped
documentation:
---
param column: column that is used for the grouping (together with the Request.version)
param session: session for the database
param where: additional where clause
---

modules.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.request
documentation: Contains all functions that access a Request object.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.request.add_request
documentation:
Adds a request to the database. Returns the id.
\:param status_code: status code of the request
\:param session: session for the database
\:param duration: duration of the request
\:param endpoint_id: id of the endpoint
\:param ip: IP address of the requester
\:param group_by: a criteria by which the requests can be grouped
\:return the id of the request after it was stored in the database

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.request.get_date_of_first_request
documentation:
Returns the date (as unix timestamp) of the first request since FMD was deployed.
\:param session: session for the database
\:return time of the first request

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.request.get_date_of_first_request_version
documentation:
Returns the date (as unix timestamp) of the first request in the current FMD version.
\:param session: session for the database
\:param version: version of the dashboard
\:return time of the first request in that version

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.auth.get_user
documentation:
Validates the username and password and returns an User-object if both are valid.
In case the User-table is empty, a user with default credentials is added.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.versions.get_versions
documentation:
Returns a list of length 'limit' with the versions that are used in the application
\:param session: session for the database
\:param endpoint_id: only get the version that are used in this endpoint
\:param limit: only return the most recent versions
\:return: a list of tuples with the versions (as a string) and dates, from oldest to newest

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.versions.get_first_requests
documentation:
Returns a list with all versions and when they're first used
\:param session: session for the database
\:param limit: only return the most recent versions
\:param endpoint_id: id of the endpoint
\:return list of tuples with versions

modules.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database
documentation:
Creates the database.
For information about how to access the database via a session-variable, see: session_scope()

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.session_scope
documentation:
When accessing the database, use the following syntax:
\>>> with session_scope() as session:
\>>> session.query(...)
\:return: the session for accessing the database.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.row2dict
documentation:
Converts a database-object to a python dict.
This function can be used to serialize an object into JSON, as this cannot be
directly done (but a dict can).
\:param row: any object
\:return: dict

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.User

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.User.username
documentation: Username for logging into the FMD.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.User.password_hash
documentation: Hashed password.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.User.is_admin
documentation: False for guest permissions (only view access). True for admin permissions.
Table for storing user management.

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.TelemetryUser

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.TelemetryUser.id
documentation: Unique anonymous identifier to group the data received through telemetry

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.TelemetryUser.times_initialized
documentation: For checking the amount of times the app was initialized

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.TelemetryUser.last_initialized
documentation: Check when was the last time user accessed FMD

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.TelemetryUser.monitoring_consent
documentation: If user agrees to share data 1 - not responded 2 - declined 3 - accepted
Table for storing a unique identifier of an FMD user

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Endpoint

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Endpoint.name
documentation: Name of the endpoint.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Endpoint.monitor_level
documentation: 0 - disabled, 1 - performance, 2 - outliers, 3 - profiler + outliers

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Endpoint.time_added
documentation: Time when the endpoint was added.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Endpoint.version_added
documentation: Version when the endpoint was added into the DB.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Endpoint.last_requested
documentation: Time when the endpoint was last requested.
Table for storing information about the endpoints.

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Request

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Request.endpoint
documentation: The endpoint that handles the request.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Request.duration
documentation: Processing time of the request in milliseconds.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Request.time_requested
documentation: Moment when the request was handled.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Request.version_requested
documentation: Version when the request was handled.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Request.group_by
documentation: Criteria which can be used for grouping multiple requests.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Request.ip
documentation: IP address of the requester.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Request.status_code
documentation: HTTP status code of the request.
Table for storing measurements of requests.

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Outlier

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Outlier.request
documentation: Request of the outlier.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Outlier.request_header
documentation: HTTP headers of the request.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Outlier.request_environment
documentation: HTTP environment of the request.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Outlier.request_url
documentation: Request URL.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Outlier.cpu_percent
documentation: CPU percent of the request at the moment of handling the request.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Outlier.memory
documentation: Memory utilization of the server when handling the request.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.Outlier.stacktrace
documentation: Stacktrace of the request.
Table for storing information about outliers.

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CodeLine

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CodeLine.filename
documentation: Filename that contains the line.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CodeLine.line_number
documentation: The line_number itself.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CodeLine.function_name
documentation: The function that contains this line.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CodeLine.code
documentation: The actual text (white spaces are stripped).

Table for storing the text of a StackLine.
This is a quadruple (filename, line_number, function_name, code) that uniquely
identifies a line in the code.

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.StackLine

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.StackLine.request
documentation: Request that belongs to this stack_line.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.StackLine.code
documentation: Corresponding codeline.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.StackLine.position
documentation: Position in the flattened stack tree.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.StackLine.indent
documentation: Level in the tree.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.StackLine.duration
documentation: Time spend in this specific code_line.
Table for storing lines of execution paths of calls.

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CustomGraph

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CustomGraph.title
documentation: Title of this graph.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CustomGraph.time_added
documentation: When the graph was first added to the dashboard.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CustomGraph.version_added
documentation: Version when the graph was added.
Table for storing custom graphs names.

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CustomGraphData

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CustomGraphData.graph
documentation: Graph for which the data is collected.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CustomGraphData.time
documentation: Moment when the data is collected.

class_variables.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.CustomGraphData.value
documentation: Actual value that is measured.
Table for storing data collected by custom graphs.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.count_group.count_rows_group
documentation:
Count the number of rows of a specified column
\:param session: session for the database
\:param column: column to count
\:param criterion: where-clause of the query
\:return list with the number of rows per endpoint

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.count_group.get_value
documentation:
---
param default: returned if the name was not found in the list
param name: 'name to filter on, e.g.: if name == a, it returns b'
param tuples: 'must be structured as: [(a, b), (c, d), ..]'
---

\:return value corresponding to the name in the list.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.count_group.count_requests_group
documentation:
Return the number of hits for all endpoints (possible with more filter arguments).
\:param session: session for the database
\:param where: additional arguments

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.count_group.count_requests_per_day
documentation:
Return the number of hits for all endpoints per day.
\:param session: session for the database
\:param list_of_days: list with datetime.datetime objects.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.outlier.add_outlier
documentation:
Adds an Outlier object in the database.
\:param session: session for the database
\:param request_id: id of the request
\:param cpu_percent: cpu load of the server when processing the request
\:param memory: memory load of the server when processing the request
\:param stacktrace: stack trace of the request
\:param request: triple containing the headers, environment and url

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.outlier.get_outliers_sorted
documentation:
Gets a list of Outlier objects for a certain endpoint, sorted by most recent request time
\:param session: session for the database
\:param endpoint_id: id of the endpoint for filtering the requests
\:param offset: number of items to skip
\:param per_page: number of items to return
\:return list of Outlier objects of a specific endpoint

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.outlier.get_outliers_cpus
documentation:
Gets list of CPU loads of all outliers of a certain endpoint
\:param session: session for the database
\:param endpoint_id: id of the endpoint
\:return list of cpu percentages as strings

modules.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.main
documentation:
This file can be executed for developing purposes.
To run use:

```pycon
>>> python main.py
```

Note: This is not used when the flask_monitoring_dashboard
is attached to your flask application.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.main.on_the_minute
documentation: dashboard.config.database_name = 'mysql+pymysql://user:password@localhost:3306/db1'
dashboard.config.database_name = 'postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/mydb'

modules.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers
documentation: This directory is used for implementing the logic between the database and the API.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.outliers.get_outlier_graph
documentation:
---
param endpoint_id: id of the endpoint
param session: session for the database
return: a list with data about each CPU performance
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.outliers.get_outlier_table
documentation:
---
param endpoint_id: id of the endpoint
param offset: number of items to be skipped
param per_page: maximum number of items to be returned
param session: session for the database
return: a list of length at most 'per_page' with data about each outlier
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.endpoints.get_endpoint_overview
documentation:
---
param session: session for the database
return: A list of properties for each endpoint that is found in the database
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.endpoints.get_endpoint_users
documentation:
---
param endpoint_id: id for the endpoint
param session: session for the database
param users: a list of users to be filtered on
return: a list of dicts with the performance of each user
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.endpoints.get_endpoint_versions
documentation:
---
param endpoint_id: id for the endpoint
param session: session for the database
param versions: a list of version to be filtered on
return: a list of dicts with the performance of each version
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.endpoints.get_api_performance
documentation:
---
param endpoints: a list of endpoints, encoded by their name
param session: session for the database
return: for every endpoint in endpoints, a list with the performance
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.endpoints.set_endpoint_rule
documentation:
---
param endpoint_name: name of the endpoint
param monitor_level: integer, representing the monitoring-level
param session: session for the database
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.versions.get_2d_version_data
documentation:
---
param column: column from the Request table
param column_data: a is of the other column
param endpoint_id: id of the endpoint
param session: session for the database
param versions: a list of versions
return: a dict with 2d information about the version and another column
---

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.versions.get_multi_version_data
documentation:
---
param endpoints: |-
  a list of all endpoints for which the data must be
  collected (represented by their name)
param session: session for the database
param versions: a list of versions
return: a 2d list of data
---

modules.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.endpoint
documentation: Contains all functions that access an Endpoint object

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.endpoint.get_num_requests
documentation:
Returns a list with all dates on which an endpoint is accessed.
\:param session: session for the database
\:param endpoint_id: if None, the result is the sum of all endpoints
\:param start_date: datetime.date object
\:param end_date: datetime.date object
\:return list of dates

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.endpoint.group_request_times
documentation:
Returns a list of tuples containing the number of hits per hour
\:param datetimes: list of datetime objects
\:return list of tuples ('%Y-%m-%d %H:00:00', count)

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.database.endpoint.get_users
documentation:
Returns a list with the distinct group-by from a specific endpoint. The limit is used to
filter the most used distinct.
\:param session: session for the database

\:return a list with the group_by as strings.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.requests.get_status_code_distribution
documentation:
Gets the distribution of status codes returned by the given endpoint.

```
param session

session for the database

param endpoint_id

id for the endpoint

return

A dict where the key is the status code and the value is the fraction of requests
```

that returned the status
code. Example: a return value of `{ 200: 0.92, 404: 0.08 }` means that status code 200 was
returned on 92% of the
requests. 8% of the requests returned a 404 status code.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.requests.get_status_code_frequencies
documentation:
Gets the frequencies of each status code.

```
param session

session for the database

param endpoint_id

id for the endpoint

param criterion

Optional criteria used to file the requests.

return

A dict where the key is the status code and the value is the fraction of requests that returned the status
```

code. Example: a return value of `{ 200: 105, 404: 3 }` means that status code 200 was returned 105 times and
404 was returned 3 times.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.controllers.requests.get_error_requests
documentation:

```

session for the database

Optional criteria used to file the requests.

```

modules.name: flask_monitoringdashboard
documentation:
The app tracks the performance of various endpoints over time.
To bind, use the following lines of code:

```pycon
>>> import flask_monitoringdashboard as dashboard
>>> from flask import Flask
    ...
>>> app = Flask(__name__)
    ...
>>> dashboard.bind(app)
```

The dashboard with the results that are collected can be found at:

: localhost:5000/dashboard

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.loc
documentation: Get the current location of the project.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.bind
documentation:
Binding the app to this object should happen before importing the routing-
methods below. Thus, the importing statement is part of this function.

```
param app

the app for which the performance has to be tracked

param schedule

flag telling if the background scheduler should be started

param include_dashboard

flag telling if the views should be added or not.
```

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.add_graph
documentation:
Add a custom graph to the dashboard. You must specify the following arguments:

```
param title

title of the graph (must be unique)

param schedule

dict containing values for weeks, days, hours, minutes, seconds

param func

function reference without arguments

param trigger

str|apscheduler.triggers.base.BaseTrigger trigger: trigger that determines when
func is called
```

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.add_database_pruning_schedule
documentation:
Add a scheduled job to prune the database of Request and optionally CustomGraph data older than the specified

```
param weeks_to_keep

number of weeks to keep in the database

param delete_custom_graph_data

flag telling if CustomGraph data should be deleted as well

param schedule

dict containing cron schedule values
```

  \- rules: all rules for this endpoint (encoded as a JSON-list)
  \- total_hits: number of hits
  \- url: link to this endpoint
---

modules.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core
documentation:
Core files for the Flask Monitoring Dashboard. It contains the following packages:
- config handles the configuration-file
- forms handles generating and processing WTF-forms
- plot handles the plotly library
- profiler handles profiling requests

It also contains the following files
- auth.py handles authentication
- colors.py handles color-hash
- group_by.py handles the group_by functionality
- info_box.py handles the generation of information box, one for each graph
- measurements.py contains a number of wrappers, one for each monitoring level
- rules.py includes a function that retrieves all rules of the entire Flask
  application, or for a specific endpoint.
- timezone: handles utc-timezone <==> local-timezone
- utils: for other functions

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.config.Config

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.config.Config.__init__
documentation: Sets the default values for the project.

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.config.Config.inject_dependencies
documentation:
Injects certain dependencies into the Monitoring Dashboard for better integration.
\:param get_ip: a function that gets the appropriate client IP address inside a request

> context. Overriding this allows you to provide the correct IP for logging purposes
> in case your setup is running behind reverse proxies.

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.config.Config.init_from
documentation:
The config_file must at least contains the following variables in section 'dashboard':
\- APP_VERSION: the version of the app that you use. Updating the version helps in

> showing differences in execution times of a function over a period of time.

- GIT = If you're using git, then it is easier to set the location to the .git-folder,
  : The location is relative to the config-file.
- BLUEPRINT_NAME: The name of the blueprint the FMD adds to the Flask app.
  : default: "dashboard"
- CUSTOM_LINK: The dashboard can be visited at localhost:5000/\{CUSTOM_LINK}.
- MONITOR_LEVEL: The level for monitoring your endpoints. The default value is 3.
- OUTLIER_DETECTION_CONSTANT: When the execution time is more than this constant \*
  : average, extra information is logged into the database. A default value for this
    variable is 2.5.
- SAMPLING_PERIOD: Time between two profiler-samples. The time must be specified in ms.
  : If this value is not set, the profiler continuously monitors.
- ENABLE_LOGGING: Boolean if you want additional logs to be printed to the console.

Default value is False
\- BRAND_NAME: The name displayed in the Dashboard Navbar.
Default value is 'Flask Monitoring Dashboard'
\- TITLE_NAME: The name displayed in the browser tab.
Default value is 'Flask-MonitoringDashboard'
\- DESCRIPTION: The text displayed in center of the Dashboard Navbar.
Default value is 'Automatically monitor the evolving performance of Flask/Python web services'
\- SHOW_LOGIN_BANNER: Boolean if you want the login page to show the 'Flask Monitoring Dashboard' logo and title.
Default value is True
\- SHOW_LOGIN_FOOTER: Boolean if you want the login page to show a link to the official documentation.
Default value is True

The config_file must at least contains the following variables in section
'authentication':
\- USERNAME: for logging into the dashboard, a username and password is required. The

> username can be set using this variable.

- PASSWORD: same as for the username, but this is the password variable.
- SECURITY_TOKEN: Used for getting the data in /get_json_data

The config_file must at least contains the following variables in section 'database':
\- DATABASE: Suppose you have multiple projects where you're working on and want to

> separate the results. Then you can specify different database_names, such that the
> result of each project is stored in its own database.

- TABLE_PREFIX: A prefix to every table that the Flask-MonitoringDashboard uses, to
  : ensure that there are no conflicts with the user of the dashboard.

The config_file must at least contains the following variables in section
'visualization':
\- TIMEZONE: The timezone for converting a UTC timestamp to a local timestamp.

> for a list of all timezones, use the following:
>
> ```pycon
> >>> import pytz  # pip install pytz
> >>> print(pytz.all_timezones)
> ```

- COLORS: A dictionary to override the colors used per endpoint.

```
param file

a string pointing to the location of the config-file.

param envvar

a string specifying which environment variable holds the config file
location.

param log_verbose

flag to print the location of the config file.
```

The settings can be changed by setting up a config file. For an example of a config file,
see config.cfg in the main-directory.

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.profiler.util.string_hash.StringHash.hash
documentation:
Performs the following reduction:

hash('abc') ==> 0
hash('def') ==> 1
hash('abc') ==> 0

```
param string

the string to be hashed

return

a unique int for every string.
```

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.profiler.util.string_hash.StringHash.unhash
documentation:
Opposite of hash.

unhash(hash('abc')) == 'abc

```
param hash

string to be unhashed

return

the value that corresponds to the given hash
```

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.profiler.util.path_hash.PathHash._encode
documentation:
Encoded fn and ln in the following way:

: \_encode(fn, ln) => hash(fn):ln

```

filename (string)

param ln

linenumber (int)

return

String with the hashed filename, and linenumber
```

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.profiler.util.path_hash.PathHash._decode
documentation:
Opposite of _encode

Example: _decode('0:12') => ('fn1', 12)

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.profiler.util.path_hash.PathHash.get_indent

Compute the amount of callers given a path.

\:return: an integer

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.profiler.util.path_hash.PathHash.get_code
documentation:
---
param path: only take the last tuple of the path. the last part contains the code
  line,
---

but hashed.
\:return: the line of code, based on the given path

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.profiler.util.path_hash.PathHash.get_stacklines_path
documentation:
Used for grouping multiple requests
\:param stack_lines: list of StackLine objects.
\:param index: index in the stack_lines, so 0 \<= index < len(stack_lines)
\:return: the StackLinePath that belongs to the given index

Used for encoding the stacktrace.
A stacktrace can be seen by a list of tuples (filename and linenumber): e.g. [(fn1, 25),
(fn2, 30)]
this is encoded as a string:

    encoded = 'fn1:25->fn2->30'

However, the filename could possibly contain '->', therefore the filename is hashed into a
number.
So, the encoding becomes:

    encoded = '0:25->1:30'

---
param endpoint_name: if specified, only return the available rules to that endpoint
return: A list of the current rules in the attached Flask app

classes.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.profiler.stacktrace_profiler.StacktraceProfiler

methods.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.profiler.stacktrace_profiler.StacktraceProfiler.run
documentation:
Continuously takes a snapshot from the stacktrace (only the main-thread). Filters
everything before the endpoint has been called (i.e. the Flask library).
Directly computes the histogram, since this is more efficient for performance
:return:

Used for profiling the performance per line code.
This is used when monitoring-level == 2 and monitoring-level == 3

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.auth.admin_secure
documentation:
When the user is not logged into the system, the user is requested to the login page.
There are two types of user-modes:
\- admin: Can be visited with this wrapper.
\- guest: Cannot be visited with this wrapper.
\:param func: the endpoint to be wrapped.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.auth.secure

Fetches the IP address from a text file hosted on GitHub and caches the result.

Args:
file_url (str): URL to the raw version of the GitHub hosted text file containing the IP address.

Returns:
str: IP address and port as a string, or None if unable to fetch.

modules.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.measurement

Contains all functions that are used to track the performance of the flask-application.
See init_measurement() for more detailed info.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.measurement.init_measurement

This should be added to the list of functions that are executed before the first request.
This function is used in the config-method in __init__ of this folder
It adds wrappers to the endpoints for tracking their performance and last access times.

functions.name: flask_monitoringdashboard.core.group_by.recursive_group_by
documentation:
Returns the result of the given argument. The result is computed as:
- If the argument is a primitive (i.e. str, bool, int, ...) return its value.
- If the argument is a function, call the function.
- If the argument is iterable (i.e. list or tuple), compute the result by iterating over the
    argument
Return type is always a string
